features.summary: Microsoft reported Q2 FY25 revenue of $69.6 billion, up 12% year-over-year, with strong performance driven by cloud and AI offerings. Microsoft Cloud revenue surpassed $40 billion for the first time, growing 21%, while the AI business reached a $13 billion annual revenue run rate, up 175%. Azure grew 31% with 13 points from AI services, though non-AI services faced execution challenges in scale motions. Commercial bookings increased 67% driven significantly by OpenAI's Azure commitments, and Microsoft 365 Copilot showed accelerated adoption with usage intensity up 60% quarter-over-quarter.
features.management_outlook: Management expects continued strong demand for cloud and AI offerings in Q3, with Microsoft Cloud revenue growth driven by Azure's 31-32% expected growth in constant currency. They anticipate being AI capacity constrained through Q3 but expect to be roughly in line with near-term demand by end of FY25 due to significant capital investments. For FY26, capital expenditure growth rates will be lower than FY25 with spending mix shifting toward short-lived assets more correlated to revenue growth. The company maintains confidence in double-digit revenue and operating income growth for the full fiscal year.
features.risks: Microsoft faces ongoing AI capacity constraints limiting near-term revenue potential, with demand continuing to exceed available capacity through Q3. The company is experiencing execution challenges in non-AI Azure services, particularly in scale motions reaching customers through partners, which may impact growth through the second half of the fiscal year. Foreign exchange headwinds are expected to decrease total revenue growth by two points in Q3, representing roughly $1 billion impact compared to October guidance assumptions.
features.key_drivers: AI services growth of 157% year-over-year is the primary revenue driver, supported by expanding data center capacity and infrastructure investments totaling $22.6 billion in capital expenditures. Microsoft 365 Copilot adoption is accelerating across enterprise customers with expanding seat counts and usage intensity, while the broader Microsoft 365 Commercial suite continues showing strength in E5 migrations. The OpenAI partnership remains strategic with new large Azure commitments providing significant bookings growth, and the company's fungible cloud infrastructure investments position it to serve growing customer contracted backlog of nearly $300 billion in remaining performance obligations.
